map.condition: Recurrence of the form T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)
Quick Sort worst-case (sorted/reverse-sorted input with first/last pivot)
Merging two sorted arrays of sizes m and n

formulas.name: Master Theorem (3 Cases)
formulas.latex: T(n) = aT\!\left(\tfrac{n}{b}\right) + f(n),\quad a \ge 1,\, b > 1 \\ \text{Case 1: } f(n) = O(n^{\log_b a - \varepsilon}) \Rightarrow T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a}) \\ \text{Case 2: } f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a}) \Rightarrow T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log n) \\ \text{Case 3: } f(n) = \Omega(n^{\log_b a + \varepsilon}) \Rightarrow T(n) = \Theta(f(n))
formulas.plain: T(n)=aT(n/b)+f(n). Case1: f=O(n^(log_b(a)-e)) => Theta(n^log_b(a)). Case2: f=Theta(n^log_b(a)) => Theta(n^log_b(a)*logn). Case3: f=Omega(n^(log_b(a)+e)) => Theta(f(n)).
formulas.whenToUse: Solve divide-and-conquer recurrences of the form T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)

terms.means: factor by which input size is reduced
cost of divide and combine steps

formulas.trap: Case 3 also requires the regularity condition af(n/b) <= cf(n) for some c < 1; gaps between f(n) and n^(log_b a) must be polynomial, not just logarithmic.

formulas.name: Critical Exponent (log_b a)
formulas.latex: \text{critical exponent} = \log_b a
formulas.plain: critical exponent = log_b(a)
formulas.whenToUse: Compare f(n) against n^(log_b a) to determine which Master Theorem case applies

terms.means: logarithm of a to the base b

formulas.trap: Confusing log base: log_2 7 ≈ 2.807, not 2.5; always compute the exact base.

formulas.name: Merge Sort Recurrence
formulas.latex: T(n) = 2T\!\left(\tfrac{n}{2}\right) + O(n) \Rightarrow T(n) = O(n \log n)
formulas.plain: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n) => O(n log n)
formulas.whenToUse: Standard merge sort on an array of n elements (all cases: best, average, worst)
formulas.trap: Merge sort is O(n log n) in ALL cases (best, average, worst) — unlike Quick Sort which is O(n^2) in the worst case.

formulas.name: Merge Sort on Strings
formulas.latex: \text{Sorting } n \text{ strings each of length } n: O(n^2 \log n)
formulas.plain: Sorting n strings of length n: O(n^2 log n)
formulas.whenToUse: When merge sort is applied to a list of n strings each of length n (comparison cost is O(n) per string)

terms.means: number of strings and also the length of each string

formulas.trap: Each comparison of two length-n strings costs O(n), so the total cost multiplies by an extra factor of n: O(n log n) * O(n) = O(n^2 log n).

formulas.name: Two-Way Merge Sort Passes
formulas.latex: \text{Number of passes} = \lceil \log_2 n \rceil
formulas.plain: Number of passes = ceil(log2(n))
formulas.whenToUse: Determine how many passes straight two-way merge sort makes on n elements
formulas.trap: After the k-th pass, sorted runs of length 2^k are formed; GATE questions often ask for the state after a specific pass, requiring simulation.

formulas.name: Merge Sort Time Scaling
formulas.latex: \frac{T(n_2)}{T(n_1)} = \frac{n_2 \log n_2}{n_1 \log n_1}
formulas.plain: T(n2)/T(n1) = (n2 log n2) / (n1 log n1)
formulas.whenToUse: Estimate maximum solvable input size given time for a known input (e.g. 30 s for n=64, find n for 6 min)

terms.means: time for input of size n_i

formulas.trap: Do not use n^2 scaling — merge sort is O(n log n), not O(n^2).

formulas.name: Quick Sort Worst-Case Recurrence
formulas.latex: T(n) = T(n-1) + O(n) \Rightarrow T(n) = O(n^2)
formulas.whenToUse: Pivot always lands at one extreme (already sorted or reverse-sorted input with first/last element as pivot)
formulas.trap: Sorted or reverse-sorted input triggers worst case only when the pivot is the first or last element; with median-of-three pivot this is avoided.

formulas.name: Quick Sort Best and Average Case
formulas.latex: T(n) = 2T\!\left(\tfrac{n}{2}\right) + O(n) \Rightarrow O(n \log n) \quad (\text{best / average})
formulas.plain: Best/Average: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n) => O(n log n)
formulas.whenToUse: Pivot splits array roughly in half on average
formulas.trap: Average case is O(n log n) even for random input, but a specific adversarial input can still force O(n^2).

formulas.name: Quick Sort Worst-Case Input
formulas.latex: \text{Last element as pivot} \Rightarrow \text{sorted } [1,2,\ldots,n] \text{ gives worst case} \\ \text{First element as pivot} \Rightarrow \text{sorted or reverse-sorted gives worst case}
formulas.plain: Last pivot: sorted [1..n] is worst case. First pivot: sorted or reverse-sorted is worst case.
formulas.whenToUse: Identify inputs that maximise Quick Sort comparisons for a given pivot selection rule
formulas.trap: For last-element pivot, the SORTED array (not reverse-sorted) is the worst case; students often confuse the two.

formulas.name: Quick Sort Comparison Count (Worst Case)
formulas.latex: C_{\text{worst}}(n) = \frac{n(n-1)}{2} = O(n^2)
formulas.plain: C_worst(n) = n(n-1)/2 = O(n^2)
formulas.whenToUse: Exact worst-case comparison count for Quick Sort (pivot always produces 0 : n-1 split)
formulas.trap: This is the maximum number of comparisons; average is approximately 1.386 n log n.

formulas.name: Quick Sort Space Complexity
formulas.latex: \text{Space: } O(\log n) \text{ average (stack)},\quad O(n) \text{ worst case (stack)}
formulas.plain: Space: O(log n) average stack depth, O(n) worst case stack depth.
formulas.whenToUse: Evaluate Quick Sort's auxiliary space requirement (recursion stack)
formulas.trap: Quick Sort is in-place for comparisons but NOT O(1) space due to the recursion stack; worst case stack depth is O(n).

formulas.name: Merging Two Sorted Lists
formulas.latex: \text{Merge sizes } m \text{ and } n: \text{comparisons} = O(m+n),\quad \text{worst} = m+n-1
formulas.plain: Merge(m, n): comparisons = O(m+n), worst case = m+n-1
formulas.whenToUse: Merging two sorted arrays/lists into one sorted array of size m+n
formulas.trap: Worst case is m+n-1 comparisons (not m+n); the last element requires no comparison because the other list is exhausted.

formulas.name: Optimal Merge Pattern (Greedy)
formulas.latex: \text{Minimum total comparisons} = \sum_{i} (\text{size}_i) \times (\text{depth}_i) \text{ using Huffman-style merge}
formulas.plain: Optimal merge: combine two smallest lists first (greedy / Huffman tree). Total cost = sum(size_i * depth_i).
formulas.whenToUse: Minimise total comparisons when merging k sorted sequences of different sizes

terms.means: number of times list i is merged (level in the merge tree)

formulas.trap: Always merge the two SMALLEST lists first; merging arbitrarily can give suboptimal total cost.

formulas.name: Bitonic Array Definition
formulas.latex: A[1..n] \text{ is bitonic if } \exists\, i: A[1] \le \cdots \le A[i] \ge \cdots \ge A[n]
formulas.plain: A[1..n] is bitonic if there exists i such that A[1] <= ... <= A[i] >= ... >= A[n] (ascending then descending).
formulas.whenToUse: Identify or search within an array that first increases then decreases

terms.means: peak index of the bitonic array

formulas.trap: The peak element can be at either end, making the array purely ascending or purely descending — these are valid bitonic arrays.

formulas.name: Search in Bitonic Array
formulas.latex: \text{Find peak: } O(\log n) \text{ (binary search on slope)} \\ \text{Search key: } O(\log n) \text{ (binary search on each half)} \\ \text{Total: } O(\log n)
formulas.plain: Find peak in O(log n), then binary search each half in O(log n). Total = O(log n).
formulas.whenToUse: Search for a key in a bitonic array using divide and conquer
formulas.trap: A naive linear scan is O(n); the divide-and-conquer approach achieves O(log n) by first finding the peak index.

formulas.name: Binary Search Recurrence
formulas.latex: T(n) = T\!\left(\tfrac{n}{2}\right) + O(1) \Rightarrow T(n) = O(\log n)
formulas.plain: T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1) => O(log n)
formulas.whenToUse: Divide-and-conquer algorithm that halves input and does O(1) work at each level
formulas.trap: Master theorem Case 1: log_2(1) = 0, so f(n) = O(1) = O(n^0); compare carefully with n^(log_b a) = n^0 = 1 — this is Case 2.

formulas.name: Strassen Matrix Multiplication
formulas.latex: T(n) = 7T\!\left(\tfrac{n}{2}\right) + O(n^2) \Rightarrow T(n) = O(n^{\log_2 7}) \approx O(n^{2.807})
formulas.whenToUse: Strassen's divide-and-conquer algorithm for n x n matrix multiplication (better than naive O(n^3))

terms.means: approximately 2.807, the exponent for Strassen's algorithm

formulas.trap: log_2 7 ≈ 2.807, NOT 2.5; standard matrix multiplication is O(n^3) while Strassen achieves O(n^2.807).

formulas.name: Linear Divide Recurrence
formulas.latex: T(n) = T\!\left(\tfrac{n}{2}\right) + O(n) \Rightarrow T(n) = O(n)
formulas.whenToUse: Algorithm that does O(n) work and recurses on half the input (e.g., selection in a sorted structure)
formulas.trap: Master theorem Case 3 applies here; geometric series n + n/2 + n/4 + ... = 2n = O(n).

formulas.name: Divide and Conquer Paradigm
formulas.latex: \text{1. Divide: split into } a \text{ subproblems of size } n/b \\ \text{2. Conquer: solve recursively} \\ \text{3. Combine: merge results in } f(n) \text{ time}
formulas.plain: 1. Divide into a subproblems of size n/b. 2. Conquer recursively. 3. Combine in f(n) time.
formulas.whenToUse: Classify whether an algorithm follows the divide-and-conquer design technique
formulas.trap: Quick Sort's combine step is O(1) (no merge needed); Merge Sort's combine step is O(n) (merge step). Both are divide and conquer.

formulas.name: Recursion Tree Depth
formulas.latex: \text{Depth of recursion tree} = \log_b n \quad (\text{input halved by factor } b \text{ each level})
formulas.plain: Recursion tree depth = log_b(n) levels
formulas.whenToUse: Count levels in the recursion tree for a divide-and-conquer algorithm reducing by factor b

terms.means: divisor by which subproblem size shrinks

formulas.trap: Total work is (work per level) * (number of levels) only when work per level is constant; if work per level changes, sum the geometric/arithmetic series explicitly.
